sentences.text: That it's these 19 Polish Jews who stopped for a second to listen to the shofar that inspired me always to these feelings and thoughts that the Jewish nation has such a huge credit card up in heaven that, if for no other reason, but 19 Jews had the guts in 1944 to stop in Bergen-Belsen from their labors and listen to the shofar, that's enough credit before all the Heavenly authorities that the final redemption to the Jewish nation should come.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They have no more excuse to refrain, to withhold this blessing from the Jewish nation.
They really suffered enough.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We also had the secret holiday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And just for the Rabbi of Satmar, they took one of these three-tiered carts out into the yard and put on the top of it straw.
And this was his [INAUDIBLE].
Of course, the winds came, the rain came, blew it over.
So it lasted one day.
So there was even a semblance of this.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One night in December-- again, these things they always did at middle of the night, at 12:00 at night-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they come around.
We have to pack like at the time of deportation.
We are going to be freed.
It was [NON-ENGLISH] holiday, I believe it was December 20th or 21st.
Again, be ready at 6:00 in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we are going to be freed.
They did not tell us where we are going.
OK, everyone puts their meager possessions together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we line up in the yard to be freed.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then they announce the railroad station, which is [INAUDIBLE], is very far away.
And by then, there was a lot of snow.
It was not in June.
They said, listen, let the adults walk.
And let the children stay, and we'll take them by truck.
So people thought this might be a good idea.
They're freeing us.
They're letting us go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how it really happened.
The adults went ahead.
And the children were left behind.
And they told us at 12:00 noon-- they left us with some adults to take care of us.
At 12:00 noon, the trucks will come to take us to the railroad section.
A lot, a lot.
We were the only camp with families.
And Jews have a lot of families.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I will not exaggerate if I'll say that about 35% of the camp population were children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Probably more, probably more, but a large children population, huge.
This was the only camp in the whole history of the [INAUDIBLE] that the children remained with the family unit.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we waited.
It's 12 o'clock.
And there are no trucks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they say that, you know, they change their mind.
The children should also walk.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a long, bitter walk.
It was in the snow.
It's not-- it wasn't like coming.
I think it was probably between 8 to 10 miles in the snow.
And my little brother then, he was five years old.
Most of the time I carried him on my shoulder.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We got there to our families, it was already 7:00 PM, pitch dark.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were going crazy.
They thought that they were deceived, and that they took the children away.
Everybody was crying, and hugging.
And it was unbelievable.
So this was the railroad station.
So where are the trains?
There is no train there.
And this was the middle of December in north Germany, freezing, but utterly freezing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember the rabbi of Satmar stood next to me.
And he's-- the man who took care of him, what they called his [NON-ENGLISH] hugged him all night from the cold.
His name was-- his name is, should be well, [?
Yosef ?] Rabbi [?
Yosef ?] Ashkenazi.
He lives in [?
Monrona. ?] Finally, 8 o'clock in the morning, the train arrives.
For the first time, we started to believe that it's real.
It's a passenger train.
It's not cattle cars, a real passenger train.
We got on the train.
I was not heated.
But for the first time, we believed it's real.
It took three days to reach the Swiss border.
We arrive midnight to Switzerland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We've arrived at the border town of St.
Gallen, remember that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We thought we are in Heaven.
We got on a Swiss train that took us from the border to St.
Gallen.
This train had two qualities that we are just not used to.
It had light.
It was well-lit.
And it was warm.
It had real steam.
We got to St.
Gallen.
And from the railroad station, they took us to some school.
And as we walked, a man who obviously heard that this group is coming, and he broke through the lines and gave an apple to the Rabbi of Satmar.
This was very big news, that the Rabbi of Satmar got an apple.
That's probably the most precious present he ever got in all his life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed here for a couple of days.
We slept on straw, but it was warm at least.
Then they took us to French Switzerland, near a city called Montreux.
And we stayed in various hotels.
They assigned hotels on the mountains above Montreux.
We want to a place called Caux, to a place called Lausanne to place called Chamby.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Swiss were not too happy we are there-- that we were there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They really didn't like us.
As a matter of fact, they wanted to get rid of us but fast.
And they said that they want to send us to Uganda.
But this time, you know, we already learned our lesson a little bit.
And we said, nothing doing.
We are not going to Uganda.
You cannot do this.
We said, if the Swiss want to send us to Uganda, there's only one way they can do it.
Everyone on that day is going to stay in his bed, in pajamas.
And they'll have to take every Jew on a stretcher and put him on a train, and send him to Uganda.
There's no-- and we are going to invite the newspapers, also.
So they changed their mind.
But to the very last day, they did not give us any freedom of movement.
We need a permit to leave, to come and go.
We were like semi-incarcerated.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
The Jews, the local Jews, I'm sorry, but they were not much better.
Hardly anyone came to visit us, to see us, to befriend us.
There was a rabbi in Montreux who possibly visited us once or twice.
His name is Rabbi [?
Botchko. ?] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They gave us 500 certificates to go to Palestine.
That was the first time there was discord in our group. 1,850 people, and there are 500 certificates.
This is 1946 already.
How are you going to distribute this?
So that was the time that [?
Castner ?] and those who were in charge of the group said, listen, we saved everybody's life.
But now, the Zionists comes first.
Those who were active in Zionist organizations all these years, they should get their reward now.
There was a very big fights over this.
And certain things that happened that are better not even said.
But these 500 people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to, at that time, to Palestine.
And that's when the Rabbi of Satmar also went on Aliyah And-- although, he was not a Zionist.
But he was very anxious to go on Aliyah.
I'm stating this specially, because people should know.
And that's when his family also-- also went.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They separated the children from the adults.
They said it's good for these kids who were in Bergen-Belsen to be separate.
And they sent me to a place called Institute Ascher, in Bex.
That was a luxury place where rich kids went from all over Europe.
I tell you, I hated it.
I found out eventually that the one who ran that was a sadist, which I did not know.
I found that out only very recently.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
In Jerusalem, lives his daughter and son-in-law.
He's a psychologist.
And he told me point blank.
I told him, how come I didn't like a place where rich kids go to?
I hated it.
He says, I want you to know I'm sorry my father-in-law was a sadist.
They made us get up every morning at 5:30 and run in the snow, and ice cold then hot shots, intermittently.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then came time for me to be bar mitzvahed.
And that was interesting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a bar mitzvah.
And my friends got together and bought me a present.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they gave it to me in a box like they give presents here, a big box.
Then you opened a smaller and a smaller.
And this box I open up, and I see something for the first time in my life.
I never saw it before.
It was a banana.
Now, when I was in kindergarten, I saw the picture of a banana.
But I never saw a real  one.
And I take in my hand.
I want to bite into it.
And somebody, an older person there, says, that's not the way you eat it.
And I didn't know how to peel it either.
So we peeled it.
We sliced it up, a tiny slice for everyone.
That was my bar mitzvah present.
But I did have great sages.
I had about 100 rabbis who gave me a blessing at that time.
So I consider it a great and beautiful bar mitzvah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then everybody applied to go either to Israel or the United States.
And Dad said, he's applying to both.
Wherever the opportunity comes, he's going to go first.
And the first papers came from the United States.
And we left Switzerland around January '47.
It took almost three months to get here.
We first went to Italy by train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there in Genoa, where the [INAUDIBLE] took care of us.
Then they took us to a place to Livorno, or Leghorn, where we boarded a freighter.
I remember to this day that freighter.
It was called That was another horrible experience.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It took four weeks to come to United States.
Constant storms, it was winter.
My parents were very sick, seasick.
And I helped the crew with the dish washing.
And therefore, they gave me all the wine I wanted.
And my parents lived on wine for four weeks.
They couldn't take anything else.
And they were in bed for four weeks.
My mother was sure she would not make it.
And she gave me some ideas where to look for relatives when we arrived to the United States.
And finally, instead of New York, we arrived to Norfolk, Virginia, where we were very hungry, starved.
And my father asked, is there a Jewish organization for a day would take us in and give us a meal?
And the people who were in charge of the shipping arranged this-- I think some Hadassah people.
And they took us for lunch.
And they had a surprise of their life.
They gave us this soft, American bread.
You know, it's the first time we ever saw this white bread, very soft.
They said, it's bread.
We said, that's not bread.
That's cake.
And we ate and ate.
We probably ate up about five, six breads in one sitting.
They-- they couldn't get over it.
I saw their faces of amazement.
How can you eat so much bread?
But we were starved.
We came to New York.
And-- and that was our arrival.
How old were you when you arrived in New York?
It was in March.
Next month will be the anniversary, March 1947.
I was 14 years old.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] possibly, I would like to mention just one area of Bergen-Belsen I didn't say before.
A significant part of the day was what they called early morning [NON-ENGLISH], the counting.
This they did religiously, these Germans.
You had to line up.
You were sick.
You were dead.
No matter what, you had to line up, rain, snow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you-- and you lined up in a certain way.
And then they counted you, et cetera.
What remained in my heart and in my mind from those lineups were the people who stood behind me.
That's when they prayed.
The lineup took from 4:00 AM to 8:00 AM every morning.
It was four hours.
It was the most miserable part of the day.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember those prayers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The morning prayer where a Jew prays, [NON-ENGLISH].
God, you love us with so much love.
We said, [NON-ENGLISH].
Blessed be God, [NON-ENGLISH], who chose the Jewish nation with love.
I understood that.
And I would become very emotional about this.
Where is the love?
This is love?
Where is the love?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And this segment of my Bergen-Belsen experience is with me to this day.
And I mention it a few times a year, always, wherever I go.
Because in our [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] beliefs, the Holy Temple was destroyed, because of hatred without cause.
And we believe that's going to be rebuilt, because of love without cause.
And I always say in our prayer, especially during the holidays, God, you saw your nation showing you love without cause in Bergen-Belsen.
If at the [NON-ENGLISH] a Jew can say, [NON-ENGLISH], God, you love us with so much love, there is no greater expression of love without cause than for a Jew to say this in Bergen-Belsen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I thought I should mention this, because we are human beings, and feelings and emotions are part of this experience.
And then we started life in-- in the United States, in America.
Yes.
That was not easy.
I had basically a gap of almost five years.
I was basically on a third grade level.
I'm a 14-year-old boy.
I have to go to school.
So where do I fit in?
And everyone was breaking their head.
Now, to put me with eight-year-olds or nine-year-olds, how does that-- that look?
That was very trying.
That was very trying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Sometimes I felt I would never catch up.
I had very serious gaps.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But thank God, I excelled in two areas.
I got to the top of the class in spelling, because I'd learned the language from new.
And I knew how to spell better than anyone, because I had to learn a word the way it's written, not the way you say it.
And I was pretty good in math, also.
So, eventually, I caught up, as the years went by, to my class level.
There are gaps to this day.
There are certain things you can't catch up with.
For example, it's-- because I have a school, I know what we teach in elementary grades.
To this day, I'm not too good in ordinary Hebrew spelling.
So when I have to write a Hebrew document as a rabbi, I always refer to the dictionary.
I want to make sure I spell it correctly.
This is something you learn here in the third grade, fourth grade, fifth grade.
Were you asked about your experiences, what you went through, once you came to school here?
That really was mind boggling.
[NON-ENGLISH] I was amazed no one asked me.
And I just came from there.
No one, so-called debriefed me.
Not the kids, not the teachers, not the principals-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nobody, absolutely nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People looked at us as a refugee family.
We moved into East Flatbush.
I must tell you, they were unusually nice to us.
We moved into a neighborhood that was not religious.
And, again, this tradition that we started in Bergen-Belsen of working primarily with a-religious people just, by chance, continued here.
We got on very well.
And everybody had-- like, had pity on us.
But no one asked us, tell your story.

It was not until [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 25 years later-- probably more, 28 years later-- until about '73, '74 that I was asked to speak about the Shoah, about the Holocaust, and went around to various schools, and synagogues, and temples.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know if I will ever understand this phenomenon that this was a taboo subject in the American Jew society.
Until about 1970, no one discussed the Holocaust.
And I'm very pleased that today it's on the forefront, it's on the front lines.
And large cities are competing with each other in building Holocaust memorials and museums.
And it's becoming part and parcel of curriculums.
And there's an awareness.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I-- I-- I have no way to understand why, in the first 25 years after the Holocaust, nobody cared about it.
It absolutely-- it was a dead subject [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the world, and even in our Jewish community.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was, like, dormant-- totally dormant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's possible we needed more time.
I only know that that period is as vivid in front of my eyes as things that happened yesterday, if not more.
Because it was so-- so traumatic.
And I'm pleased that finally the world is going to know it.
Because in Bergen-Belsen we really had two fears.
One, that we'll never get out alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And two, that they will kill us and no one's going to know about it.
That was even worse than getting killed.
And that's the reason I think this is proper.
It's only right as a religious Jew or just even as a plain Jew.
I think this is a proper opportunity to thank God for his great mercies that he enabled us to live to this day.
I think one should even recite a blessing of [NON-ENGLISH] at such an occasion.
It's the first time in-- since the Holocaust that I'm actually relating this story to an official body.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Over there, we were sure we would not live.
And-- and no one would ever find out about it.
The Germans camouflaged everything.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I consider this a great moment in my life to be able to relate this story.
And I think I should also express my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] most deep-felt gratitude [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to
Right, for a moment, you know, I get so excited.
To Mr.
Steven Spielberg, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I consider him a [NON-ENGLISH].
I think that God will give him [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] unlimited rewards for what he did.
He did an unusual kindness to the dead, that they are not forgotten, with the film Which, by the way, is precise.
It's exactly the way it happened.
I did not see in it an iota of artistic license or exaggeration.
That's exactly the way it happened in every country.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God should bless him for that.
And to enable the survivors to convey the story is a second kindness on his part.
When you go through something like this, you want the world to know, and especially these tragic events that happened to our nation without precedent in all of history.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would likely, really, to take this opportunity to thank Mr.
Spielberg.
You should know you are a big [NON-ENGLISH], and God will bless you.
In Bergen-Belsen, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everyone said if we ever get free, we will devote our lives for the well-being of the Jewish nation.
I became a big Zionist.
It went deep into my heart.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For various reasons, we did not settle in Israel.
But I feel that, still, I have to do something for the Jewish people.
And that is what my life has to be.
And we have a yeshiva for unaffiliated children, children who never had the good fortune to grow up with religion, with our culture.
And we take them in from all over.
American children, Israeli children, Russian children, and lately we took in a large group of Syrian children who just arrived from Damascus.
We give them an excellent education.
And we do everything that they-- when they graduate by us, they should be imbued with our heritage and that all of them should continue to yeshiva high school and to be proud members of our nation.
Our nation has a right to live, and to exist.
And every Jew should know who he is and-- and be very proud of it.
I went back [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for the very first time to Hungary this year.
A cousin of mine took me.
And [INAUDIBLE] as I flew into Budapest, I saw the red, white, and green flag at the Budapest airport.
All these dreadful feelings and events came back.
I thought, I'm back in 1944.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we were on a bus.
And I was together with much younger boys and girls.
And we went to visit the graves of the forefathers.
And these boys put on cassettes, this happy music.
And they clapped their hands.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as we went by these cities, it came back to my mind, these were cities where there was vibrant Jewish life, rich Jewish life.
Many of our cousins were rabbis there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I just couldn't take the music, and the happiness, and the joy.
I told the girls, you know, girls, it's so kind of you to take me with you.
But I cannot take the music.
I'm getting sick in my stomach.
